nativeRange.habitat: The Horseface Loach inhabits sandy-bottomed rivers, streams, and seasonal floodplains across mainland Southeast Asia. In the wild it favors lowland rivers with moderate to fast current and fine sand or silty substrates, and is commonly found buried in sand with only the head exposed.

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (mandatory for burrowing)
smooth river rocks and rounded pebbles
driftwood for shelter and visual breaks
low, broad-leaved plants anchored to driftwood or rocks (Anubias, Java fern)
moderate water flow replicating river conditions

diet.notes: In the wild the Horseface Loach feeds primarily on small invertebrates, insect larvae, worms, and crustaceans sifted from sandy riverbeds. In the aquarium it generally accepts sinking pellets, frozen or live bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, daphnia, and earthworm fragments, and will also graze on organic detritus and algae. Sand-sifting is a natural foraging behaviour, and meaty foods such as bloodworms tend to elicit the strongest feeding response. Spirulina-based sinking wafers can be offered to round out the diet.

goodWith: medium to large tetras and barbs (not fin-nippers)
other loaches of similar size (botia, yasuhikotakia)
avoidWith: very small fish or nano fish that may be outcompeted
bottom-dwelling fish requiring the same sandy territory (can cause stress through competition)
species sensitive to the burrowing disturbance of the substrate

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Acantopsis dialuzona (closely related, often traded as horseface loach); verify species-level accuracy before use.
Wikimedia image labelled A. dialuzona; species is commonly confused in trade with A. rungthipae. Visually similar; suitable as placeholder until species-confirmed photograph is available.

summary: The Horseface Loach is a slender, elongated fish from Southeast Asian rivers, named for its distinctively long, horse-like snout. It reaches up to 20 cm and is a dedicated sand-burrower, spending much of its time completely buried in fine substrate. Its strict requirement for deep, fine sand and sensitivity to poor water quality place it at an intermediate care level.
careNotes:
The Horseface Loach (Acantopsis rungthipae) is one of several closely related species sold under the "horseface" or "horsehead" trade name. A taxonomic revision published in 2017 split the previously single-species group Acantopsis choirorhynchos into multiple valid species — including A. rungthipae, A. dialuzona, and A. spectabilis — meaning that trade specimens may belong to any of these. Accurate identification requires close examination of scale-row counts and fin positions, though all share the same husbandry needs in an aquarium setting.

Deep, fine sand is the single most important husbandry requirement. These fish bury themselves completely, leaving only the eyes and snout visible, using this behaviour for both rest and ambush feeding. A minimum of 8 cm of smooth, fine-grained sand (0.1–0.5 mm particle size) is strongly recommended; coarser gravel causes abrasion to the delicate snout and belly, and will stress or injure the fish. Bare-bottom tanks are wholly unsuitable.

They originate from the Mekong drainage and associated river systems of mainland Southeast Asia (Thailand, Laos, Vietnam, Cambodia, Myanmar), preferring warm (24–30°C), slightly soft to moderately hard, mildly acidic to neutral water. Good oxygenation and moderate flow replicating the riverine environment are beneficial. Water quality must be maintained carefully — they are sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes.

Horseface loaches are largely crepuscular to nocturnal and may rarely be seen during bright daylight unless a consistent feeding schedule is established. Keeping them in groups of three or more reduces stress; solitary specimens tend to hide excessively. They can be kept with peaceful mid-water fish but do best with open sandy floor space. They are not fin-nippers and pose no threat to tankmates beyond substrate disturbance during burrowing.

Feeding should include sinking meaty foods (bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp) offered at dusk or with lights dimmed. High-quality sinking pellets and wafers can supplement the diet. Avoid prolonged feeding on live tubifex from wild sources due to disease-transmission risk; frozen or irradiated alternatives are preferred.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented for Acantopsis rungthipae. No successful captive spawning protocols are established in the hobbyist or scientific literature. The species is presumed to be an egg-scatterer like related cobitid loaches, but specific triggers, courtship behaviour, and fry rearing requirements are unknown. Wild populations likely spawn seasonally in response to monsoon-driven flood pulses and cooler oxygenated water — conditions that are difficult to replicate in a home aquarium. Commercially available specimens are wild-caught or occasionally bred in Southeast Asian fish farms under conditions not fully disclosed.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Acantopsis dialuzona
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. SeriouslyFish does not yet maintain a dedicated page for A. rungthipae; the A. dialuzona entry covers the traded horseface loach complex and is husbandry-equivalent per the Boyd et al. 2017 revision. Provided temperature range, pH, hardness, minimum tank size, substrate requirements, diet, and native range data.

additional.name: FishBase — Acantopsis rungthipae
additional.notes: Cross-referenced adult size (to ~20 cm TL), native range (Mekong basin, Thailand), taxonomy (Cobitidae, Cypriniformes), and taxonomic notes on the Boyd et al. 2017 species-level revision splitting the A. choirorhynchos complex.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: General loach husbandry reference. Confirmed sand-substrate requirement, intermediate care level, social grouping recommendations, and diet guidance for the Cobitidae family. No dedicated horseface loach page available.

additional.name: Boyd, C.M. et al. (2017) — Taxonomic revision of Acantopsis
additional.notes: Primary taxonomic reference. Boyd et al. (2017) published the formal species-level split of Acantopsis choirorhynchos sensu lato into multiple valid species including A. rungthipae, A. dialuzona, and A. spectabilis. This revision is the basis for the correct identification of trade specimens and the taxonomic notes in this record.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Horseface Loach profile
additional.notes: Trade and husbandry profile confirming burrowing behaviour, sand depth requirements, social grouping, diet, and the identification challenges posed by the traded Acantopsis complex.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding has been documented for this species. It is presumed to spawn seasonally in response to flood-pulse conditions, though the specific triggers remain unknown. Commercially available specimens are wild-caught or raised in Southeast Asian fish farms under conditions that are not widely disclosed.
